skill.name: cicd-automation-workflow-automate
skill.description: Design CI/CD pipelines, GitHub Actions workflows, release automation, infrastructure automation, and developer workflow scripts with security and rollback planning.
skill.summary: Build practical CI/CD and workflow automation plans for development teams.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and secret references, but review shows they are Markdown examples for CI/CD workflows rather than hidden executable code. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The skill should publish with a warning because it teaches deployment automation, secret usage, Terraform apply, Docker pushes, and Kubernetes configuration handling.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Automation Uses Powerful Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as risky guidance, not malicious code. The playbook includes Docker builds and pushes, Terraform apply, npm installs, and documentation deployment commands that can change external systems when copied into real workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown inside Markdown examples, so they are not executed by the skill itself. They are still operationally risky because users may copy them into privileged CI/CD environments.

medium_findings.title: CI/CD Examples Reference Secrets and Tokens
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as sensitive guidance. The workflow examples use GitHub secrets for database URLs, AWS credentials, package tokens, Docker credentials, Kubernetes config, Snyk, SonarCloud, and GitHub tokens.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references use GitHub secret syntax rather than hardcoded secret values. The risk remains meaningful because the skill handles high-value deployment and scanning credentials.

medium_findings.title: Environment and Local Configuration File Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as moderate filesystem risk. The setup script copies .env.example to .env.local and prompts users to update it, which is common but can lead to local secret sprawl if not controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example does not read or exfiltrate existing secrets. It does create and direct users to edit an environment file, which should be treated carefully.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Expected Third-Party Tooling References
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking. The URLs point to example deployment endpoints, pre-commit repositories, Helm repositories, and localhost documentation rather than suspicious exfiltration services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are visible in documentation examples and match the stated CI/CD automation purpose. No evidence found of hidden outbound collection or encoded payload transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The scanner flagged wording around CI/CD, guides, and generated documentation as weak cryptographic algorithms, but the reviewed locations do not contain MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom crypto use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct review of the cited lines shows CI/CD descriptive text and documentation output settings. No evidence found of weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Cache Directory Detections Are Benign Workflow Cache Paths
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for hidden home directory access. The examples cache ~/.npm and ~/.cache in GitHub Actions, which is normal dependency caching and not secret harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are used by actions/cache in a CI example and do not target credential files. No evidence found of reading private keys or shell history.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged CI/CD Shell Steps
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include shell commands for Docker image publishing, Terraform apply, Helm deployment, and global package installation. Users should review privileges, branch conditions, and approvals before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous behavior is visible in workflow examples, not in an auto-running script. It is still a realistic risk if copied without environment protections.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Passed Into Workflow Steps
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use secret values in deployment, testing, publishing, Kubernetes, and scanning steps. This is expected for DevOps automation but requires least-privilege tokens and log redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax uses CI secret stores, which is safer than literals. The overall pattern remains sensitive because credential misuse in CI can affect production systems.

content.value_statement: Manual release and deployment processes create drift, delays, and inconsistent quality gates. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design repeatable CI/CD workflows with testing, security checks, approvals, and rollback notes.
actual_capabilities: Plans CI/CD pipeline stages, triggers, artifacts, caches, and quality gates.
Drafts GitHub Actions and release automation patterns for common project workflows.
Suggests security scanning, secret handling, approval, and notification steps.
Outlines Terraform, Kubernetes, monitoring, documentation, and dependency update automation.
Produces rollout, rollback, risk, and assumption notes for automation changes.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a validated pipeline for every repository.
Generated workflow steps need human review before production deployment.
Secrets, cloud permissions, and compliance rules must be configured outside the skill.
Examples may need version updates for actions, tools, and platform APIs.

use_cases.title: Create a New CI Pipeline
use_cases.description: Define build, test, lint, coverage, and artifact stages for a repository that lacks automated checks.

use_cases.title: Standardize Releases
use_cases.description: Design semantic versioning, release notes, package publishing, and documentation update workflows.

use_cases.title: Add Deployment Controls
use_cases.description: Add environment gates, Terraform plans, security scans, notifications, and rollback notes to deployment workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Map Current Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this repository structure and list the current build, test, release, and deployment steps that should be automated.

prompt_templates.title: Design GitHub Actions Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow plan for this project with triggers, caching, tests, artifacts, security scans, and required secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Release Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a release automation process with semantic versioning, changelog generation, package publishing, documentation updates, and rollback checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this deployment workflow and propose safer approvals, least-privilege secrets, Terraform plan gates, smoke tests, monitoring, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: A Node.js service needs pull request checks and a main branch deployment workflow.
output: Pipeline stages covering install, lint, unit tests, integration tests, build, image scan, and artifact upload.
Deployment plan with environment gates, smoke tests, notifications, and rollback notes.
List of required secrets and permissions to review before enabling the workflow.

output_examples.input: A team wants automated releases from conventional commits.
output: Release workflow outline with dependency installation, semantic release, changelog updates, package publishing, and release notes.
Branch and token requirements for stable, beta, and alpha release channels.
Risk notes for protected branches, package registry permissions, and failed publish recovery.

output_examples.input: A platform team needs Terraform automation for pull requests and main branch changes.
output: Plan and apply flow with formatting, validation, pull request comments, and main branch apply controls.
Secrets and backend configuration checklist for cloud credentials and state storage.
Approval, audit, and rollback recommendations for infrastructure changes.

best_practices: Review all generated workflow files before enabling write, deploy, or cloud permissions.
Use least-privilege secrets, protected environments, and required approvals for production steps.
Test automation in a non-production branch or environment before rollout.
anti_patterns: Do not copy deployment examples into production without adjusting permissions and rollback plans.
Do not store secrets in repository files, logs, artifacts, or generated documentation.
Do not let CI workflows run privileged commands on untrusted pull request code.

faq.question: Does this skill execute deployments automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow designs and examples. Users must choose whether to create or run any pipeline files.

faq.question: Which tools does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on GitHub Actions, release automation, Terraform, Kubernetes, monitoring, documentation, dependency updates, and security scanning.

faq.question: Can it help with secrets?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify required secrets and suggest safer handling, but users must configure secrets in their own CI platform.

faq.question: Is it safe for production workflows?
faq.answer: It can support production planning, but all generated steps need review, protected environments, and rollback validation.

faq.question: Can it modernize an existing pipeline?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review current stages and suggest caching, artifacts, quality gates, security scans, and release improvements.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
